Off Calendar Jul-11-2023 Text Ruling

MOTION FOR LEAVE TO File An Amended Complaint
Off Calendar May-18-2023 Granted Per Written Order
MINI MINUTES:

HEARING RE: PLAINTIFF PROOFPOINT, INC.'s Motion for Leave to File An Amended Complaint.

No appearances made in this matter. The Court adopts its tentative ruling.

Plaintiff Proofpoint Inc's motion for leave to file an amended complaint is GRANTED.

"The court may, in furtherance of justice, and on any terms as may be proper, allow a party to amend any pleading or proceeding by adding or striking out the name of any party, or by correcting a mistake in the name of a party, or a mistake in any other respect; and may, upon like terms, enlarge the time for answer or demurrer. The court may likewise, in its discretion, after notice to the adverse party, allow, upon any terms as may be just, an amendment to any pleading or proceeding in other particulars; and may upon like terms allow an answer to be made after the time limited by this code." (Code Civ. Proc., Sec. 473, subd. (a)(1), emphasis added.) "The trial court has discretion to allow amendments to the pleading 'in the furtherance of justice.' This discretion should be exercised liberally in favor of amendments, for judicial policy favors resolution of all disputed matters in the same lawsuit." (Kittredge Sports Co. v. Superior Court (1989) 213 Cal.App.3d 1045, 1047.)

Preliminarily, the court finds Proofpoint has substantially satisfied the requirement of California Rules of Court, rule 3.124(b). (See Doshi Decl. at 2.)

Abnormal argues the motion should be denied in part because several proposed amendments are irrelevant, vague, improper, futile and/or prejudicial. The preferrable practice is to permit the amendment and allow the parties to test an amended complaint's legal sufficiency by demurrer, motion for judgment on the pleadings or other appropriate proceedings. (Kittredge Sports Co., Cal.App.3d at 1048.) The court will permit the proposed amendment, and the parties may test the amended complaint in due course.

Abnormal argues that leave to amend would be prejudicial because Proofpoint has allegedly evaded discovery obligations. Prejudice exists where "the amendment would result in a delay of trial, along with loss of critical evidence, added costs of preparation, increased burden of discovery, etc." (Edmon & Karnow, California Practice Guide: Civil Procedure Before Trial 6:656 (Rutter: 2022).) On this record, the court does not find that amendment would be prejudicial to the extent that leave to amend should be denied.

Finally, Abnormal does not oppose amendment to allege the API theory, but it argues that the court should also postpone the trial by six months. (See (Code Civ. Proc., Sec. 473, subd. (a)(2) ["When it appears to the satisfaction of the court that the amendment renders it necessary, the court may postpone the trial,..."].) Generally, this department cannot continue trial dates. Here, trial is still five months away, giving the parties time, if necessary, to request a continuance in the civil master calendar department, Department 206.

Order signed.

Judge: Joseph M. Quinn; Clerk: Sean Kane; Not Reported. (302/JMQ)

HEARING RE: PLAINTIFF PROOFPOINT, INC.'s Motion To Compel Defendant Abnormal Security Corporation's Further Responses To Proofpoint's First Set Of Special Interrogatories And Requests For Production Of Documents And For Sanctions.

Both sides stipulate to the matter being decided by the pro tem judge. The pro tem judge and counsel for both sides (all appearing remotely) authorize the clerk to sign the formal stipulation form on their behalf.

Matter argued and submitted. The Court adopts its tentative ruling with modifications, as follows:

The Motion is GRANTED as to Special Interrogatories 1, 22, 23, and 26 and Requests for Production 1, 3, and 28 (but only in part as to 28). The Motion is otherwise DENIED.

Abnormal Security Corporation ("Abnormal") shall serve amended discovery responses consistent with this Order that provide information and documents through June 16, 2022.
Amended responses to the Special Interrogatories shall be served within 30 days from service of Notice of Entry of this Order. Substantial compliance as to the Requests for Production shall be made within 45 days from service of Notice of Entry of this Order (any production of documents made after 45 days is not to exceed 50%).

As to Special Interrogatory 23, Abnormal is not required to list the recruitment dates for individuals who had left more than three years prior to being recruited.

As to Special Interrogatory 26, Abnormal is not required to identify individuals where the only reason Abnormal believes such individuals possess knowledge or information relevant to the claims or allegations in the Complaint is because of communications with Abnormal's attorneys. As to Request for Production 28, Abnormal is only required to produce documents concerning employees whose prior employer was Proofpoint, Inc.

Counsel for plaintiff is to prepare and submit a proposed order to the opposing side for approval as to form, and then submit the proposed order to the pro tem judge at mkahn@gibsondunn.com for review/signature.

HEARING RE: DEFENDANT ABNORMAL SECURITY CORPORATION'S DEMURRER TO COMPLAINT.

Matter argued and submitted. The Court adopts its tentative ruling.

Defendant's demurrers (on preemption grounds) are overruled. The trade secret law does not displace claims that are "independent and based on facts distinct from the facts that support the misappropriation claim," even if those claims are "related to trade secret misappropriation." (Angelica Textile Services, Inc. v. Park (2013) 220 Cal.App.4th 495, 506.) The complaint pled such facts. (See, e.g., 2:4-24.)

Order signed.
